title: 17 Tiqimt — St. James the Apostle & The Assembly
narrative:
Tiqimt

St. James the Apostle, Son of Zebedee (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Hosea the Prophet

St. Andrew of Crete, Archbishop and Hymnographer

On the seventeenth day of Tiqimt, the monthly commemoration of St. James the son of Zebedee brings the first of the apostolic martyrs into the month of interiority with a particular message: the cup. 'Are you able to drink the cup that I drink?' Jesus asked James and his brother John when they requested the seats of honor beside him in the Kingdom. James said yes without knowing what yes meant, and his life was the slow discovery of its meaning — the gradual, interior formation that prepared him for a death so quick and so public that the Acts of the Apostles dispenses with it in a single verse: Herod killed James the brother of John with the sword. The brevity of the execution notice, after the long investment of the apostolic formation, is itself Tiqimt's characteristic meditation: the interior preparation is long; the external testimony can be short; but what was formed in the interior is not diminished by the brevity of its outer expression.

St. Hosea the Prophet brings one of the most intimate and most painful of the prophetic books into the day's assembly. Hosea's prophecy came to him through his own marriage — through the command to marry a woman who would prove unfaithful, so that his life would become a living parable of God's relationship with Israel. The prophet who speaks most profoundly of divine love does so from within a broken covenant, from the position of the abandoned spouse who will not abandon the unfaithful one, who redeems rather than divorces, who speaks tenderly to the faithless beloved: 'I will allure her, and bring her into the wilderness, and speak tenderly to her heart.' In the month of the Covenant of Mercy, Hosea is the prophet whose biography is the theology — whose suffering marriage is the divine mercy's most unguarded self-disclosure.

St. Andrew of Crete, the Archbishop of Gortyna and one of the greatest liturgical poets of the Eastern Church, is the author of the Great Canon of Repentance — a monumental work of penitential hymnody that walks through the entire Old and New Testaments, holding each figure before the soul as a mirror in which the individual sees her own condition. The Great Canon, sung in its entirety on a Thursday of the Great Lent, is the interior life's fullest liturgical expression: an extended act of self-examination conducted through the saints and sinners of scripture, in which the anonymous 'my soul' is addressed and challenged and consoled across 250 troparia of searching and tender verse. Andrew of Crete in Tiqimt's assembly is the hymnographer of the interior — the one who gave the Church's contemplative tradition its most sustained literary form.

James, Hosea, and Andrew of Crete together on the seventeenth day of Tiqimt compose a meditation on the interior formation that produces the exterior witness. James's yes to the cup was an interior event before it was a historical one; the cup had to be accepted in the soul before the sword could find the body. Hosea's prophecy was an interior wound before it was a prophetic text; the broken marriage had to be lived before it could be spoken. Andrew's Canon was an interior journey before it was a liturgical text; the penitential searching had to be done before the poetry could be written. The Covenant of Mercy in Tiqimt forms its witnesses from the inside out, and the seventeenth day is the month's reminder that the inside work is always the prior work.
